title: Te Kete Uru Rākau: The Basket of Forest Knowledge | Ngā Kete Mātauranga o Te Waonui-a-Tāne
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore the traditional knowledge systems of Ngāti Kahungunu through the lens of Te Waonui-a-Tāne (the great forest of Tāne) that surrounds Mangakōtukutuku College. The lesson connects to local pūrākau (stories) of Tāne separating Ranginui and Papatūānuku, and how this created the biodiversity of our local forest ecosystem that has sustained our hapū for generations.
learningObjectives: Identify and name at least 10 native forest species using their correct Māori names and describe their traditional uses
Construct simple sentences in te reo Māori explaining the relationship between forest biodiversity and Māori cultural practices
Create a digital whakapapa (genealogy) chart showing the connections between Tāne Mahuta and local forest species
Participate in a wānanga (learning session) with local kaumātua using appropriate tikanga and te reo Māori greetings

activities.title: He Hīkoi i Te Ngahere - Forest Walk and Identification
activities.description: Guided walk through the Mangakōtukutuku forest reserve with local kaumātua. Students use laminated identification cards with Māori names, scientific names, and traditional uses of native plants. They record findings in a digital journal using te reo Māori vocabulary for colors, sizes, and locations.

activities.title: Ngā Kōrero o Te Waonui-a-Tāne - Storytelling Workshop
activities.description: Kaumātua share pūrākau about Tāne Mahuta and the creation of the forest. Students work in pairs to create simple digital stories using Book Creator, incorporating Māori names of forest species and basic sentence structures learned in class.

activities.title: Te Whakapapa o Te Ngahere - Forest Genealogy Mapping
activities.description: Using Canva or similar tools, students create visual whakapapa charts showing the relationship between Tāne Mahuta and specific native species found in our local forest. Each connection includes a Māori phrase explaining the relationship.

activities.title: He Mahi Rongoā - Traditional Medicine Preparation
activities.description: Practical session where students learn to identify and prepare simple rongoā (traditional medicines) from approved forest plants under expert guidance. They label all materials with Māori names and uses, following tikanga protocols.

activities.title: Te Wānanga ki te Marae - Marae Learning Experience
activities.description: Visit to local marae where students present their learning to kaumātua using te reo Māori phrases and receive feedback. Includes practicing proper marae etiquette and participating in poroporoaki (farewell ceremony).

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua experts from local marae
Te Ara Encyclopedia of New Zealand - Māori Forest Lore section
Local forest reserve access permits and safety equipment

tasks: Digital forest species identification quiz with Māori names
Recorded 2-minute te reo Māori explanation of one plant's traditional use
Completed whakapapa chart with minimum 10 forest species connections
Peer assessment of marae visit participation using tikanga rubric

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 4: Use basic Māori patterns and structures to communicate about immediate environment
Social Sciences: Identity, Culture, and Organization - Level 4: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
Science: Living World - Level 4: Explore how Māori scientific knowledge and Western science knowledge systems interact
The Arts: Visual Arts - Level 4: Explore Māori visual art forms and their cultural contexts
